en.extract:
The brown teal (Anas chlorotis; Māori: pāteke) is a species of dabbling duck of the genus Anas native to New Zealand. It was long considered conspecific with the flightless Auckland and Campbell teals as Anas aucklandica; the name "brown teal" has also been largely applied to that entire taxon. Common in the early years of European colonisation, the species was heavily harvested as a food source. Its numbers quickly fell, especially in the South Island, and in 1921 it became fully protected. Captive breeding and releasing into predator-controlled areas was successful in establishing localised re-introduced populations after 2003.


Description
There are no distinctive differences between a male, female and a juvenile brown teal during non-mating season. They all have a white ring around their eyes as well as a mottled brown colour on their heads and throat. During breeding season the male will begin to change colour, now having a green coloured head, chestnut coloured breast and a white stripe on each side of their body. They will sometimes also have a white clerical neck band. This does vary as some males do tend to be more colourful than others.


Taxonomy
The species is considered ancestral to the flightless Auckland teal and Campbell teals, which are now recognised as separate species based on geographic isolation, morphology, and genetics. They are still sometimes referred to as being part of the "brown teal group". The use of the name 'pāteke' is now common and is specific for this particular species.
Concerns have been raised about establishing genetically similar populations during re-introduction. The original captive pateke were sourced from Great Barrier Island, where only one haplotype was found in abundance, compared with eleven haplotypes found in the other remaining population at Mimiwhangata. The newly released flocks exclusively contain these birds of captive origin, and diversity is perhaps now not being fully represented in the National population.
The former population of Fiordland brown teal, which died out by 2013 has been replaced with captive-origin birds. Sequencing showed striking similarity to grey duck and mallard sequences, with which they group. It therefore seems extremely likely that the former Fiordland population of brown teal have at sometime in the past hybridised extensively with these invading species.


Behaviour
The brown teal is largely nocturnal in habit by dabbling duck standards. This is an evolutionary response to natural diurnal predators such as the New Zealand falcon, Eyles' harrier, or skuas further south in their range. Brown teal have no defense against introduced cats, dogs, stoats & ferrets, which can kill adults & ducklings, or against rats which eat eggs.


Diet

It feeds by dabbling and upending, like its relatives. Its diet consists mainly of aquatic invertebrates like insects and their larvae, or crustaceans. It appears quite fond of molluscs. Small species such as pipi (Paphies australis) and large wedge shell (Macomona liliana) are eaten whole and crushed in the gizzard. For feeding on larger cockles such as Austrovenus stutchburyi (New Zealand cockle), at least some New Zealand teals have developed a peculiar technique, as of now undocumented in other birds, to force their rather soft bills between the cockle shells and tear out the flesh with a jackhammer-like pumping motion. At night brown teal will forage on land some distance from the streams used as a refuge during the day (Worthy 2002).


Breeding
Nest of dry grass near water or under shelter of large Carex, heavily lined with down. A clutch of four to eight creamy-brown eggs is laid. Incubation is done by only the female and takes 27–30 days. The male stays in his territory as a guard, aggressive to all other waterfowl.


Distribution and conservation
This species is endangered and occurs predominantly on offshore islands but also in predator-proof sanctuaries on the mainland such as Tawharanui Regional Park. Formerly, it was widespread on the New Zealand mainland, but it disappeared there due to introduced predators like cats, dogs and rats, which easily preyed on this unwary, weakly flying bird. According to the IUCN categorization as VU D1, fewer than 1000 adult birds remain. The species has recently been upgraded to endangered by Birdlife International (Birdlife 2007), and the change will be reflected in the next update of the IUCN red list.
Apart from Fiordland's Arthur Valley, there is only one other site in the South Island where brown teal live in the wild: since 2017, the Department of Conservation and Project Janszoon have released just under 300 of the waterfowl in the Awapoto River in Abel Tasman National Park. The birds are successfully breeding in that location.


Footnotes


References

BirdLife International (2007) Species factsheet: Anas chlorotis. Downloaded from Archived 2007-07-10 at the Wayback Machine on 5/9/2007
Moore, Suzanne J. & Battley, Phil F. (2003): Cockle-opening by a dabbling duck, the Brown Teal. Waterbirds 26(3): 331–334. DOI:10.1675/1524-4695(2003)026[0331:CBADDT]2.0.CO;2 PDF fulltext
Worthy, T.H. & Holdaway, R.N. (2002) The Lost World of the Moa, Indiana University Press:Bloomington, ISBN 0-253-34034-9


Further reading
O'Brien, Rory M. (1990). "Anas chlorotis Brown Teal" (PDF). In Marchant, S.; Higgins, P.G. (eds.). Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic Birds. Volume 1: Ratites to ducks; Part B, Australian pelican to ducks. Melbourne, Victoria: Oxford University Press. pp. 1289–1295. ISBN 978-0-19-553068-1.


External links

S.M. O'Connor; R.F. Maloney; R.J. Pierce (2007). "Pateke (Anas chlorotis) recovery plan, 2005–10 (Threatened Species Recovery Plan 59)" (PDF). Department of Conservation, Wellington, New Zealand. Retrieved 2007-09-03.
Brown Teal Site

The '''brown teal''' ('''''Anas chlorotis'''''; {{langx|mi|pāteke}}) is a [[species]] of [[dabbling duck]] of the genus ''[[Anas]]'' native to New Zealand. It was long considered [[conspecific]] with the flightless [[Auckland teal|Auckland]] and [[Campbell teal]]s as ''Anas aucklandica''; the name "brown teal" has also been largely applied to that entire [[taxon]]. Common in the early years of European colonisation, the species was heavily harvested as a food source. Its numbers quickly fell, especially in the South Island, and in 1921 it became fully protected. Captive breeding and releasing into predator-controlled areas was successful in establishing localised re-introduced populations after 2003.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021"/>

==Description==
There are no distinctive differences between a male, female and a juvenile brown teal during non-mating season. They all have a white ring around their eyes as well as a mottled brown colour on their heads and throat.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.brownteal.com/brown-teal-pateke/brown-teal-description/|title=Brown Teal Description|date=2013-03-28|website=Brown Teal / Pateke Recovery Program|access-date=2020-02-14}}</ref> During breeding season the male will begin to change colour, now having a green coloured head, chestnut coloured breast and a white stripe on each side of their body. They will sometimes also have a white clerical neck band. This does vary as some males do tend to be more colourful than others.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.ducks.org/hunting/waterfowl-id/blue-winged-teal|title=Blue-winged Teal {{!}} Types of Ducks & Geese|website=www.ducks.org|access-date=2020-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thespruce.com/teal-duck-profile-386920|title=What Type of Duck is a Teal?|website=The Spruce|language=en|access-date=2020-02-14}}</ref>
[[File:Brown teal (Anas chlorotis) chick Tiritiri Matangi.jpg|thumb|Chick]]
==Taxonomy==
The species is considered ancestral to the flightless [[Auckland teal]] and [[Campbell teal]]s, which are now recognised as separate species based on geographic isolation, morphology, and genetics. They are still sometimes referred to as being part of the "brown teal group".<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/brown-teal|title = Brown teal | New Zealand Birds Online}}</ref> The use of the name 'pāteke' is now common and is specific for this particular species.{{cn|date=March 2026}}

Concerns have been raised about establishing genetically similar populations during re-introduction. The original captive pateke were sourced from Great Barrier Island, where only one [[haplotype]] was found in abundance, compared with eleven haplotypes found in the other remaining population at Mimiwhangata. The newly released flocks exclusively contain these birds of captive origin, and diversity is perhaps now not being fully represented in the National population.<ref>Bowker-Wright, Gemma; Loss of Genetic Diversity with Captive Breeding and Re-Introduction: A Case Study on Pateke/Brown Teal (Anas chlorotis) 2008</ref>

The former population of Fiordland brown teal, which died out by 2013<ref>Hyslop, L. & Kenny, K. http://www.stuff.co.nz/the-press/national/9396503/South-Island-duck-extinct</ref> has been replaced with captive-origin birds. Sequencing showed striking similarity to grey duck and mallard sequences, with which they group. It therefore seems extremely likely that the former Fiordland population of brown teal have at sometime in the past hybridised extensively with these invading species.<ref>Gemmell, NJ., Flint, H.J., 2000. Taxonomic status of the brown teal (Anas chlorotis) in Fiordland. Conservation Advisory Science Notes, No. 326, Department of Conservation, Wellington</ref>

==Behaviour==
The brown teal is largely [[nocturnal]] in habit by dabbling duck standards. This is an [[evolution]]ary response to natural [[Diurnality|diurnal]] predators such as the [[New Zealand falcon]], [[Eyles' harrier]], or [[skua]]s further south in their range. Brown teal have no defense against introduced cats, dogs, stoats & ferrets, which can kill adults & ducklings, or against rats which eat eggs.

==Diet==
[[File:Anas chlorotis.jpg|thumb|Brown Teal (Anas chlorotis) feeding]]
It feeds by dabbling and upending, like its relatives. Its diet consists mainly of [[aquatic animal|aquatic]] [[invertebrate]]s like [[insect]]s and their larvae, or [[crustacean]]s. It appears quite fond of [[molluscs]]. Small species such as pipi (''[[Paphies australis]]'') and large wedge shell (''[[Macomona liliana]]'') are eaten whole and crushed in the gizzard. For feeding on larger [[Cockle (bivalve)|cockle]]s such as ''[[Austrovenus stutchburyi]]'' (New Zealand cockle), at least some New Zealand teals have developed a peculiar technique, as of now undocumented in other birds, to force their rather soft bills between the cockle shells and tear out the flesh with a [[jackhammer]]-like pumping motion. At night brown teal will forage on land some distance from the streams used as a refuge during the day (Worthy 2002).

==Breeding==
Nest of dry grass near water or under shelter of large ''[[Carex]]'',<ref>Perrine Moncrieff; New Zealand Birds and How to Identify Them</ref> heavily lined with down. A clutch of four to eight creamy-brown eggs is laid.<ref>M.J. Williams, BSc (Hons), PhD</ref> Incubation is done by only the female and takes 27–30 days. The male stays in his territory as a guard, aggressive to all other waterfowl.<ref>M.J. Williams, BSc (Hons), PhD</ref>

==Distribution and conservation==
This species is endangered and occurs predominantly on offshore islands but also in predator-proof sanctuaries on the mainland such as [[Tawharanui Regional Park]]. Formerly, it was widespread on the New Zealand mainland, but it disappeared there due to introduced [[predator]]s like cats, dogs and rats, which easily preyed on this unwary, weakly flying bird. According to the [[IUCN]] categorization as '''VU D1''', fewer than 1000 adult birds remain. The species has recently been upgraded to [[endangered species|endangered]] by [[Birdlife International]] (Birdlife 2007), and the change will be reflected in the next update of the IUCN red list.

Apart from Fiordland's [[Arthur River (New Zealand)|Arthur Valley]], there is only one other site in the South Island where brown teal live in the wild: since 2017, the [[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]] and Project Janszoon have released just under 300 of the waterfowl in the [[Awapoto River]] in [[Abel Tasman National Park]]. The birds are successfully breeding in that location.<ref>{{cite web |title=Pāteke / brown teal |url= https://www.janszoon.org/the-park/wildlife/pateke-brown-teal/ |access-date=16 January 2024 |work=Project Janszoon|date= 24 June 2018 }}</ref>

zh.extract:
褐鸭（台湾作棕腹鴨）（学名：Anas chlorotis），是雁形目鸭科的一种鸟类。


特征
褐鸭体重约499.09克，翼长约196.6毫米，嘴峰长约42.7毫米，喙宽度约18.2毫米，喙厚度约14.2毫米，跗蹠长约35.6毫米，尾长约77.8毫米。褐鸭在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在开放的湖泊、沼泽等湿地环境中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是水生植物。


分布
新西兰及其离岛。


参考资料
